task: Memory grows steadily even though connection count is stable. Build a per-socket payload-tracking instrumentation to find the leak.
reasoning: Closures over large payloads survive long after the relevant emit completes. Common culprits: event listeners that capture request bodies, accumulating maps keyed by socket.id but not cleaned up. Add a WeakRef-based audit that tracks total tracked bytes per socket.
why_this_is_correct: WeakMap+WeakRef lets the audit cleanup follow socket GC. FinalizationRegistry removes the map entry when the socket is collected. Per-socket byte counts surface outliers in dashboards (one socket suddenly tracking 100MB = leak point).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Strong Map<string, Audit> keyed by socket.id holds sockets alive even after disconnect — never GC'd. Tracking all events globally without per-socket scope hides which sockets are leaking. JSON.stringify of huge args is itself a leak risk; size it cheaply or sample.

task: Add tracing spans (OpenTelemetry) around every Socket.IO handler to see per-event latency in distributed traces.
reasoning: OpenTelemetry's createSpan wraps work in a span. For Socket.IO, instrument handlers via an interception layer that starts a span on event receipt, ends on handler completion. Propagate trace context from the client if sent in handshake.
why_this_is_correct: onAny intercepts every event. If an ack callback is present, wrap it to close the span when the handler responds — accurate handler latency. Without ack, end span next tick (best effort). Errors recorded so traces show failed handlers.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping every socket.on manually loses cross-cutting consistency. Without ack detection, span ends immediately — duration is microseconds, useless. No error capture = silent failures invisible in traces.

task: Wrap socket.emit on the client with the same typed event maps as the server for safety.
reasoning: Sharing typed event maps between client and server (sock-006) gives autocomplete and prevents drift. The client Socket<ServerToClient, ClientToServer> generic enforces it. Emit signatures derive from ClientToServer.
why_this_is_correct: Generic Socket type gives autocomplete on emit and on. socket.timeout(ms).emit is the built-in ack timeout helper. Wrapping in a Promise gives ergonomic async/await for handlers, with typed error vs success branches.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Untyped Socket: emit('chat:snd', text) typos compile, fail silently at runtime. Bare emit with ack callback loses timeout — hangs forever on server outage.

task: Implement WebRTC signaling over Socket.IO: offer/answer/ICE exchange between peers in a room.
reasoning: Signaling is offer → answer → ICE candidates. Server is just a relay between two peers; doesn't process SDP. Pair peers by room; relay messages targeting a peer's socket id.
why_this_is_correct: Server never touches SDP or media — just relays. Targeting by socket.id directs messages precisely. fetchSockets returns peers in the room for initial peer discovery. disconnecting hook notifies remaining peers so they can tear down the connection.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Broadcasting offers to the whole room: each new peer ICE-floods all others. Forgetting peer-left notification leaves WebRTC connections in 'connecting' state indefinitely after a peer drops.

task: API keys with scopes (read:users, write:orders) and per-tenant isolation. Verify on every request.
reasoning: API keys grant programmatic access. Each key has a tenant + scope set. Lookup key hash (never plaintext), check scope against required scope for the route.
why_this_is_correct: sha256 hash for lookup is sufficient — keys are high-entropy (no rainbow attack). Plaintext shown ONCE at creation, never stored. Scope-based authorization is route-level granular. tenantId attached to req for downstream filtering. lastUsedAt update fire-and-forget to avoid blocking the response.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Storing keys plain: DB leak = mass compromise. No scopes: every key has full access; revoking one user takes down all integrations. Forgetting tenant scoping in services: cross-tenant data leak via key misuse.

task: Service-to-service mTLS in Node: present a client cert and verify the upstream's cert against a pinned fingerprint.
reasoning: mTLS = both client AND server present certs. Add cert pinning to defeat compromised CAs: precompute the expected upstream cert SHA-256, verify on each connection.
why_this_is_correct: Default TLS chain verification AND fingerprint check — defense in depth. SHA-256 of raw DER cert is stable across renewals only if cert is identical; rotation requires updating pin. checkServerIdentity returning an error makes the connection fail immediately.
what_breaks_naive_approach: rejectUnauthorized:false: any cert accepted, mTLS purpose defeated. No fingerprint pinning: compromised CA can mint a valid cert and MITM. Pinning to the host name only (not cert): doesn't catch new cert from same CA.

task: Generate URL-safe, high-entropy tokens of arbitrary byte length without collisions in practice.
reasoning: crypto.randomBytes gives cryptographic randomness. base64url encoding is URL-safe (no +/=). 16 bytes (128 bits) is enough for most uses; 32 bytes for high-stakes tokens.
why_this_is_correct: randomBytes uses OS CSPRNG. base64url avoids URL-encoding hassles. UUIDv7 prepends millisecond timestamp so generated IDs sort chronologically — useful for DB primary keys (B-tree friendly inserts) without losing randomness.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Math.random for tokens: predictable, attackers brute-force the seed. UUIDv4 random IDs scatter B-tree inserts, hurting write throughput at scale. crypto.randomUUID is fine for v4 but loses sort-by-time.

task: Centralized log redaction: scrub credit-card numbers, JWT tokens, and known-secret patterns from log output.
reasoning: Logs end up in many places (file, aggregator, alerts). Pre-redact at the boundary so secrets never enter the pipeline. Use a layered approach: regex for known patterns, allowlist of safe fields, deny-list otherwise.
why_this_is_correct: Layered defense: key-name redaction catches structured secrets, regex catches secrets embedded in free-text fields. Depth cap prevents infinite recursion on circular structures. Lowercasing keys catches `Authorization`, `authorization`, `AUTHORIZATION`.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting field names alone misses secrets stuffed into logging strings ('Login failed for password: hunter2'). Regex without key-name check redacts only patterns. Skipping CC redaction = PCI compliance violation.

task: Outbound webhooks with at-least-once delivery, retries, and a circuit breaker so a misbehaving subscriber doesn't degrade your service.
reasoning: Webhook delivery is fire-and-forget but business-critical. Persistent queue + retry policy + per-subscriber circuit breaker. Include event id in payload so subscriber can dedupe.
why_this_is_correct: 4xx (except 408/429) = subscriber doesn't want this event; don't retry, treat as 'success' from our perspective. 5xx/timeouts retry with exponential backoff. Breaker drops further attempts when subscriber is consistently down — protects throughput. X-Event-Id header lets subscribers dedupe.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Retrying 4xx wastes our resources on permanent rejection (validation errors). No breaker: one slow subscriber consumes worker capacity. Best-effort (no queue) loses events on crash.

task: User-supplied URLs in <a href> should reject javascript: and data: URLs but allow http/https/mailto.
reasoning: React doesn't sanitize href. javascript:alert(1) in href executes on click. Validate scheme; render as plain text or remove href if invalid.
why_this_is_correct: URL parser rejects malformed and exotic schemes. Relative URLs (starting with /) are allowed for same-site links. Unsafe URLs degrade to a span (still shows the text, just not clickable). External links get noopener+noreferrer to block tabnabbing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: <a href={userUrl}>: javascript:alert(1) runs on click. Blocklist of 'javascript:' is bypassable with whitespace, case variations, or data: alternatives. Allowing arbitrary target=_blank without rel exposes window.opener.

task: Multiple instances of the same Provider in the tree (e.g., per-list state) should each have isolated stores.
reasoning: Module-level singleton store is great for global; not for per-instance state. Per-Provider store via useRef: each Provider mount creates a fresh store. Children use the closest provider's store via Context.
why_this_is_correct: useRef inside Provider creates exactly one store per Provider mount. Two <Provider> instances = two independent stores. Children resolve to the nearest provider through Context. useSyncExternalStore for concurrent-mode safety.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Module-level let store: state shared across all Provider instances. useState inside Provider: re-renders all consumers on update; no selector pattern.

task: Reducer state contains items[]; consumers commonly need indexed access. Add a memoized selector layer.
reasoning: Recomputing index from items[] on every render is wasteful. Build it once after state change in a memo or stash inside reducer state. Latter trades store size for read perf.
why_this_is_correct: Derived state lives outside the reducer (which stays pure and minimal). useMemo recomputes only when items reference changes — which only happens on dispatches that actually mutate items. byId Map gives O(1) lookups for downstream components.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Storing byId in reducer state: must keep in sync with items on every action — easy to forget on a new action. Computing inline in render: O(n) map construction every render.

task: Modal opens with focus on the first input, traps Tab inside, and restores focus on close. Pure React, no external library.
reasoning: Accessibility requirement. Find focusable elements within the modal; on Tab/Shift+Tab from last/first, wrap. Save the previously-focused element to restore on close. Use ref to the panel as the trap boundary.
why_this_is_correct: Querying focusables at the moment of Tab gives fresh list (DOM changes don't break the trap). Recomputing on every keydown is cheap. Wraps both directions (Tab and Shift+Tab). Escape closes. Focus restoration on close is essential for screen-reader users.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Querying focusables once at mount = stale list, breaks if modal content is dynamic. No focus restoration = keyboard users disoriented. Trapping focus without aria-modal=true confuses screen readers about what's in scope.

task: Subscribe to an event source on mount, unsubscribe on unmount, with proper cleanup semantics under StrictMode.
reasoning: StrictMode mounts, unmounts, then remounts in dev. Cleanup MUST be symmetric to setup — otherwise StrictMode reveals leaks (double-subscribed in production).
why_this_is_correct: Handler defined inside effect = same reference for on and off. Effect's cleanup symmetrically removes the subscription. StrictMode's mount/unmount/mount yields exactly: subscribe → unsubscribe → subscribe — no leak.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Defining handler outside the effect leaks across re-renders. Skipping cleanup means StrictMode runs you with TWO subscriptions in dev — surfaces a real bug.

task: Shadows are expensive. Configure a shadow-map setup that balances quality and FPS, including bias to fix shadow acne.
reasoning: DirectionalLight casts shadows by rendering the scene from light's POV into a depth texture. Bigger map = sharper but slower. Bias offsets depth comparison to prevent shadow acne. Normal bias helps on curved surfaces. PCF soft shadows are cheap quality win.
why_this_is_correct: 2048x2048 shadow map = balance of quality and memory. Tight orthographic frustum (15 units each direction) maximizes shadow resolution on the visible area. bias=-0.0005 + normalBias=0.05 eliminates shadow acne and peter-panning without major artifact. PCFSoftShadowMap is the soft-shadow preset that's reasonable on mid-tier GPUs.
what_breaks_naive_approach: 4096+ shadow maps: memory bandwidth bottleneck. Default loose frustum: most texels wasted, visible shadow becomes pixelated. No bias: shadow acne striping all over. Hard shadows look 2002.

task: Picking the right instance from an InstancedMesh by raycasting — out-of-the-box raycast returns the wrong index.
reasoning: Standard raycast against InstancedMesh works in recent Three.js versions and returns the instanceId. Older versions don't; for those (and for many-attribute meshes), GPU picking via a separate render pass with unique colors per instance is more reliable.
why_this_is_correct: intersectObject returns the instanceId field on hit. getMatrixAt retrieves the per-instance transform for downstream logic (e.g., highlight by replacing the matrix). Loop through meshes lets you pick from multiple instanced forests.
what_breaks_naive_approach: intersectObjects(scene.children, true) recurses but loses InstancedMesh special handling in some Three.js versions, returning the parent mesh without instanceId. Manually generating a color-per-instance picking pass works but is much more code; use only if intersectObject fails.

task: Add IBL (image-based lighting) from an HDR environment map for realistic PBR reflections.
reasoning: PBR materials need an env map for proper reflections. Raw HDR isn't filtered for PBR; use PMREMGenerator to prefilter mipmaps for varying roughness. drei's <Environment> wraps this for R3F.
why_this_is_correct: PMREMGenerator prefilters the HDR for PBR roughness levels — mipmaps used directly by Standard/Physical materials. drei's Environment hides this complexity for common presets. background:false attaches lighting without painting the HDR as skybox.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Using raw HDR as envMap: reflections look noisy, no roughness response. CubeTextureLoader for old-style cube maps loses the easy preset list. Forgetting pmrem.dispose leaks GPU resources.

task: Use R3F's onPointerOver/onPointerOut events for hover state instead of manual raycasting.
reasoning: R3F sets up a global raycaster and dispatches synthetic pointer events to meshes via onPointerOver, etc. Cheaper to write than manual raycasting; events propagate up the object graph like DOM events.
why_this_is_correct: R3F handles raycast + event dispatch automatically. stopPropagation prevents the same hover bubbling up to ancestors (common when nested meshes overlap). Cursor change is the typical visual hint.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Manual raycasting in onPointerMove fires hundreds of times/sec with extra cost. Forgetting stopPropagation: ancestors also receive hover, flickering state. Not resetting cursor on out: stuck pointer style after navigating away.

task: Attach a request-scoped trace (route, params, user) to every error using AsyncLocalStorage so it appears in logs without explicit passing.
reasoning: AsyncLocalStorage from node:async_hooks gives request-scoped context. Set at request start, read from anywhere during the request (including deep in services). Errors enriched at log time pick it up automatically.
why_this_is_correct: ALS propagates context across await boundaries — no parameter threading. enrichedError stamps the request context on any thrown error. Error middleware reads error.context for log enrichment.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Passing requestId as a parameter to every function: noise + easy to forget. Storing in a module-level variable: requests race and overwrite. Without context, errors logged from deep services have no idea which request they're tied to.

task: Recommendation API is slow/flaky — return cached results if the live call fails instead of failing the whole page.
reasoning: Graceful degradation: fall back to a stale-but-acceptable response. Implement with try/catch + cache lookup. Tag the response so client knows it's stale.
why_this_is_correct: Hard timeout (500ms) on upstream so a slow service doesn't tank latency. On failure, return cached (last good) results with a stale flag. UI can show 'Showing recent recommendations' if stale. Empty array as last resort beats 500.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Awaiting unbounded upstream: page wait 30s when service is down. No cache: page fails completely. Returning cache without flag: stale results look like fresh, masking the outage.

task: Reliable event publishing: write domain events to an 'outbox' table in the same transaction as the entity write, then publish asynchronously.
reasoning: Dual-write problem: save entity + publish to message bus in separate transactions risks one succeeding and one failing. Outbox pattern: events written to a local table in the same DB transaction (atomic). A separate publisher polls and ships to the bus, marking as published.
why_this_is_correct: Entity and outbox writes are still atomic, and drainers now claim one event atomically before publishing. The lockId guard prevents two workers from marking or publishing the same event concurrently.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Polling unpublished events without a claim lets multiple drainers publish the same row. Save-then-publish or publish-then-save still has the dual-write failure window.

task: Unit-of-work pattern: collect changes across multiple repositories and commit in one transaction.
reasoning: Multi-repo updates need transactional semantics. Pass a session/transaction handle through repos. UoW class holds the session, exposes commit/rollback. Repos accept session as last arg.
why_this_is_correct: Single session threaded through repos = atomic across documents/collections. withUnitOfWork helper enforces commit/rollback pairing. Repos remain agnostic of UoW — they just accept a session.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Each repo opens its own session: writes happen in separate transactions, partial failure leaves inconsistent state. Forgetting to abort on error leaves a stuck transaction holding locks.

task: Memory grows over hours. Trigger a heap snapshot programmatically on demand for offline analysis.
reasoning: v8.getHeapSnapshot() returns a stream you can pipe to a file. Combine with an admin endpoint (auth-gated) for on-demand capture. Snapshot inspection in Chrome DevTools reveals retainers.
why_this_is_correct: getHeapSnapshot() streams without copying — large heaps don't OOM the dump. Writes to disk so the snapshot survives even if process restarts. Admin-token gate prevents anyone triggering expensive snapshots. Snapshots load in Chrome DevTools Memory panel for retainer analysis.
what_breaks_naive_approach: writeFileSync of heap dump blocks event loop for seconds — request queue piles up. Unauthenticated endpoint = attacker freezes your service with one POST. In-memory buffering of the snapshot can OOM during the operation that exposes the leak.

task: Grid of 10x10000 cells (large CRM table) — render with windowing across both axes.
reasoning: VariableSizeGrid renders only visible rows AND columns. Pair with AutoSizer for parent dimensions. Memoize cell renderers and column widths.
why_this_is_correct: Only visible cells are mounted. Memoized callbacks avoid re-renders when grid props haven't changed structurally. truncate prevents overflow that would defeat the fixed cell size. AutoSizer handles parent resize via ResizeObserver internally.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain <table> with 10*10000 = 100k DOM nodes: blank screen, 4GB RAM. Single-axis virtualization (just rows): still 10*visible_rows cells per column count. Inline getColumnWidth recreates per render and disables react-window memoization.

task: Add an item to an array sub-document only if not already present, atomically. Use $addToSet vs $push correctly.
reasoning: $addToSet skips inserts when value already exists in the array. Atomic. For array of objects, equality is full document match — for partial match, use $addToSet only when the array is of primitives or you control all fields.
why_this_is_correct: $addToSet is ideal for primitive arrays. For object arrays where uniqueness is on a single key (not full object equality), the filter `tags.key:{$ne:key}` ensures push only when the key isn't present — atomic in a single operation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reading then pushing in JS: race condition adds duplicates. $addToSet on object arrays only deduplicates exact-match objects — two tags with same key but different value will both be added.

task: Multi-instance cron via Mongo: one instance runs a daily task. Implement leader election with a lease document.
reasoning: Instances race to acquire a lease doc with an expiry. Holder periodically extends lease. Others wait. If holder dies, lease expires and another takes over. Index on expiry.
why_this_is_correct: Atomic upsert + filter on (expired OR same-holder) makes acquisition race-free. Heartbeat at 1/3 lease prevents losing the lease during work. Conditional delete on release avoids stealing another instance's lease if our heartbeat lagged past expiry.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Acquire via 'check exists then create' = race. No heartbeat: long task loses lease mid-execution. Unconditional release: if our lease expired and another took over, we'd delete theirs.

task: Type a CSV parser so the row shape is derived from the column-header tuple at compile time.
reasoning: Given a tuple of header names, the row type is Record<HeaderName, string>. Template literals + tuple mapping. Powers ergonomic readers where headers are known statically.
why_this_is_correct: `const` modifier on H captures the readonly tuple literal type, so H[number] is the union of header strings. Row type is precise — accessing rows[0].nme (typo) fails compile. Lightweight CSV parser handles quoted fields with escape.
what_breaks_naive_approach: parseCsv returning Record<string, string>: type system can't help with header typos. Hand-written interface drifts from actual headers passed at runtime. Using line.split(',') breaks on quoted commas.

task: Replace Socket.IO's default JSON parser with msgpack for binary efficiency.
reasoning: Default parser uses JSON which is wasteful for binary-heavy payloads. @socket.io/msgpack-parser provides a drop-in MessagePack alternative. Smaller payloads, faster encode/decode for typed arrays.
why_this_is_correct: msgpack handles Buffer/Uint8Array efficiently without base64. Smaller wire size (~30% for typed data). Drop-in: must be set on BOTH client and server, otherwise parse errors. Pair with websocket-only transport for max benefit.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Different parsers on client vs server: connection fails with parse error. Using JSON for binary payloads: base64 inflation + parse cost. Mixing parsers across multiple Socket.IO servers in a fleet breaks one of them.

task: JWE: encrypted JWT for storing sensitive claims that even the user shouldn't see (server-only).
reasoning: JWS (signed) protects integrity but is readable. JWE (encrypted) protects confidentiality. Use when you need server-side state in a token without DB lookup (e.g., temporary credentials, encrypted refresh tokens).
why_this_is_correct: RSA-OAEP-256 wraps a content key; A256GCM encrypts content with integrity. Algorithms pinned to defeat downgrade. exp claim enforced by jose. Asymmetric so the public key can be shared with token issuers while only the server can decrypt.
what_breaks_naive_approach: JWS for sensitive claims: anyone reads the payload (base64 not encryption). Symmetric encryption shared across many services: breach of one leaks all. Not pinning algorithms invites algorithm-confusion attacks.

task: Endpoint serves files from a user-supplied filename. Sanitize against path traversal (..) and absolute paths.
reasoning: Naive `fs.readFile(baseDir + filename)` allows `../../etc/passwd`. Normalize and verify the resolved path stays under baseDir. Use path.resolve + startsWith check.
why_this_is_correct: path.resolve normalizes .. and . segments. Comparing against resolvedBase + sep ensures the file is inside the directory (avoids prefix-match bug where /tmp/foo would match /tmp/foobar). Null-byte check defeats poison null byte attacks on older filesystems.
what_breaks_naive_approach: path.join without resolve allows ../ to escape. Using `targetPath.startsWith(baseDir)` without trailing sep matches /tmp/foo for baseDir=/tmp/f. No null-byte check on legacy systems allows /tmp/safe\0../../../etc/passwd to trick C path APIs.

task: Centralized toast notification system: any component can push a toast; toasts render at the app root via portal.
reasoning: Singleton store + portal render. push() adds a toast with id+expiry; component auto-dismisses after duration. Stack vertically.
why_this_is_correct: useSyncExternalStore subscribes the container to module-level toast state — no provider needed. push from anywhere (services, hooks) without prop drilling. Portal escapes any z-index trap. setTimeout removes after duration.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Component-local toast state requires prop drilling or context. Provider with array of toasts re-renders the whole subtree on each push. SSR check (typeof document) avoids breaking server rendering.

task: Error Boundary at the route level: reset error when route changes so SPA navigation recovers without full reload.
reasoning: Generic ErrorBoundary catches but doesn't know when to reset. Pass route key as resetKeys; on route change, parent props change → boundary resets state.
why_this_is_correct: resetKeys with location.pathname auto-resets when user navigates. Reset button retries current route. Anchor (not Link) to home forces a full reload as ultimate escape hatch.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Error sticks until full page reload — frustrating UX. Reset triggered manually requires user finding the button. Without onError telemetry, you have no visibility into errors users see.

task: Wrap a sprawling third-party SDK (Stripe, AWS) behind a Facade interface that exposes only the operations your app needs.
reasoning: Facade pattern: simplifies a complex subsystem with a narrow interface. Stops vendor SDKs from leaking everywhere. Easier to swap providers, mock for tests, version-pin internally.
why_this_is_correct: App talks to PaymentsFacade — never to Stripe types. Switching to Adyen = write AdyenPaymentsFacade. Domain logic stays vendor-agnostic. Status mapping centralizes Stripe-specific 'refunded' vs 'status' inconsistency.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sprinkling Stripe.Charge types across services couples them to Stripe forever. Migrating providers requires touching every file that imports Stripe. Mocking Stripe in tests means stubbing dozens of methods most code doesn't use.

task: Diagnose why a memoized component still re-renders: distinguish React.memo vs useMemo vs props identity issues.
reasoning: Three layers: parent passes stable props, child wraps in memo, child's children handle their own optimization. If parent passes new objects/functions each render, memo fails. useMemo on the parent fixes that. Use React DevTools Profiler to confirm.
why_this_is_correct: Without useMemo on filter, every Parent render creates a new object — memo comparison fails. With useMemo, filter identity is stable across unrelated re-renders. useCallback stabilizes handleClick. Unrelated state change doesn't re-render FilteredList.
what_breaks_naive_approach: <FilteredList filter={{search,category}} ...>: new object each render, memo defeated, child re-renders even when irrelevant state updates. Wrapping the child in memo without addressing prop identity is theater.

task: Add a generic timeout wrapper around any Promise so it rejects after N ms.
reasoning: Use Promise.race against a setTimeout-reject. Cleanup the timer on resolution. AbortController-based version is preferred when the underlying operation supports it; this is for the simple case.
why_this_is_correct: finally clears the timer regardless of outcome — no late firing. Custom TimeoutError lets callers `catch (e) if (e instanceof TimeoutError)`. The wrapped promise still runs to completion in the background; for true cancellation, pair with AbortController.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No clearTimeout = timer fires after success, harmless but leaves a dead promise that rejects unhandled. Plain `new Error('timeout')` makes type-based catch impossible.

task: Integration test for Socket.IO handlers using socket.io-client against a real ephemeral server instance.
reasoning: Mocking Socket.IO loses behavioral coverage. Spin up an ephemeral httpServer on port 0 (random free), connect client, exercise the handler, assert via ack. Tear down after each test.
why_this_is_correct: Port 0 = OS picks a free port — no test collisions in CI. websocket-only transport speeds up handshake. forceNew=true makes each connect a fresh socket (not reused from a Manager). close shuts down both layers cleanly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hardcoded port: flaky in CI when port is taken. Reusing connections via the Manager: tests influence each other. Forgetting io.close: subsequent tests connect to a server with stale handler state.

task: Implement 'log out from all devices' by versioning the user's auth tokens.
reasoning: Add `authVersion` to user doc. JWT includes the version at issuance. Verify checks that version matches user's current. Logout-all increments the version, invalidating all existing tokens.
why_this_is_correct: Single increment invalidates every outstanding token for that user. Verification requires DB lookup (one extra read per request) — acceptable cost for the feature. Combine with a per-user cache to amortize the cost.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Maintaining a token blocklist: unbounded growth; checking on every request gets slow. Forcing all users to re-login by rotating secret: affects everyone, even users who didn't request logout.

task: Responsive images with srcset + sizes to serve the right resolution per device without overloading mobile.
reasoning: srcset lists multiple resolutions; sizes tells the browser the actual rendered size. Browser picks the right URL based on viewport and DPR. Combined with width descriptors (w) for true responsive.
why_this_is_correct: srcset+sizes lets the browser download only the resolution needed. Mobile gets 320w not 1920w — saves bandwidth and decode time. The fallback src is the largest for non-supporting browsers. CDN URL with ?w param is a common image-server pattern.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single src=1920w: mobile users download 5MB images that render at 320px. srcset without sizes: browser doesn't know rendered width, picks too big. No lazy = all images load on initial scroll.

task: Run a series of async jobs strictly sequentially (each waits for previous), without Promise.all.
reasoning: Sometimes ordering matters (database migrations, ordered events). Sequential loop via for-of + await. Don't be tempted by map(async) — that fires in parallel.
why_this_is_correct: for-of with await ensures strict serialization. Variant with fail-fast returns the index of failure so caller can resume from there. Pure helpers, no deps.
what_breaks_naive_approach: items.map(fn) returns array of promises — fires all in parallel even though map syntax suggests sequential. await items.map(fn) doesn't even await the promises because Array isn't awaitable in a useful way.

task: Strictly typed EventEmitter where on/emit are constrained by an event map.
reasoning: Node's EventEmitter is untyped. Wrap with a generic class parameterized by event map. on/emit/off enforce event-name + payload pairs.
why_this_is_correct: Generic event map constrains all method signatures. Tuple args allow multiple parameters (matching EventEmitter semantics). Wraps the standard EventEmitter under the hood so it behaves identically.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Untyped EventEmitter: on('user.create') (typo) silently never fires. emit('order.paid', 'o_1', 'wrong type') accepted at runtime. Cast-heavy patterns in event-driven code hide bugs.

task: Log every disconnect with the reason for debugging client-side issues.
reasoning: Socket.IO emits 'disconnect' with a reason: 'transport close', 'ping timeout', 'io server disconnect', etc. Log these to spot patterns (mass 'ping timeout' = network issue or server overload).
why_this_is_correct: Single reason field is the diagnostic gold. sessionDurationMs surfaces clients that disconnect immediately (auth fail loop) vs after hours (normal). Avoid logging on every event — just connect/disconnect.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No reason in log: 'user disconnected' tells you nothing. Logging per event = log spam. No duration = can't distinguish ephemeral connections (bad sign) from stable ones.

task: Layered rate limiting: per-IP for unauth, per-user for auth. Combine without double-counting.
reasoning: Per-IP catches credential-stuffing before auth; per-user catches abuse after. Apply per-IP unconditionally; switch to per-user after auth middleware sets req.userId. Distinct keys, distinct limits.
why_this_is_correct: IP limiter runs first (cheap; protects auth from brute force). User limiter runs after auth (more generous because authenticated users are legit). Separate Redis keys = no double-counting. Headers expose both limits so clients can self-regulate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Only per-IP: NAT'd corporate users hit limit collectively; per-user limiting absent for the bulk of usage. Only per-user: pre-auth endpoints unprotected from brute force.

task: Retry-safe HTTP wrapper: retry only idempotent methods (GET, HEAD, PUT, DELETE) on 5xx/network errors.
reasoning: Retrying POST is dangerous (double-creates) unless server is idempotent. Retry GET/HEAD freely; PUT/DELETE generally safe. Network errors and 5xx are retry-candidate; 4xx (except 429, 408) are not.
why_this_is_correct: maxAttempts overridden to 1 for non-idempotent methods unless caller opts in. RetryableHttpError flags status codes worth retrying. TypeError covers `fetch` network failures. AbortError (caller cancelled) never retries.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Retrying POST creates duplicates when server processed the request but response never reached client. Retrying 4xx burns attempts on permanent failures. Without status filter, retries 200 OK responses you accidentally re-throw.

task: Validation, authorization, and business-logic layered as middleware so each layer can focus on one concern.
reasoning: Common antipattern: one handler does parse + check user + load entity + check ownership + business logic. Split into middlewares that progressively enrich req. Handler is just the business call.
why_this_is_correct: Each middleware does one thing and enriches req. Handler is tiny. Permissions live in authorize where reviewers find them. Adding a new check = another middleware in the chain.
what_breaks_naive_approach: One mega-handler: 100 lines mixing parsing + auth + DB + logic. Skipping the ownership check buried inside the handler is easy and catastrophic.

task: Configure Express static file serving with proper cache headers for fingerprinted assets.
reasoning: Files with content hashes in names (main.abc123.js) can cache forever. index.html should never cache. immutable directive tells browsers to skip revalidation.
why_this_is_correct: Hashed assets get one-year immutable cache — zero revalidation, instant repeat visits. index.html is forced to revalidate so a new deploy is picked up immediately. ETag disabled for hashed files (filename IS the hash, no need to compute).
what_breaks_naive_approach: All files cached 1y including index.html: users stuck on old deploy until they manually clear cache. No cache on hashed assets: repeat visits redownload all JS/CSS. ETag on huge files spends CPU calculating hashes for no benefit on hashed names.

task: Update one element inside an array of subdocuments based on a filter, atomically, using arrayFilters.
reasoning: Positional operator $ only matches the FIRST matching element. arrayFilters let you update specific elements by criteria. Particularly useful when the filter on the parent doc differs from the filter on the array element.
why_this_is_correct: $[t] is the named identifier matched by arrayFilters. Multiple elements can match — affects all matching. Filter on parent + filter on array element are decoupled.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Positional operator $ + filter `tasks._id: taskId` works for single match but not multi-match. Without arrayFilters, you'd fetch+modify+save, losing atomicity.

task: Async Socket.IO middleware that performs a DB lookup — handle errors so they don't crash the connection silently.
reasoning: io.use accepts async middleware. Throwing inside it kills the connection silently (well, with connect_error). Always pass next(err) — never throw raw — and make errors meaningful to the client.
why_this_is_correct: try/catch around the DB call ensures errors reach next() — client gets a clear connect_error event. Logging server-side preserves the original stack for ops while not leaking it to the client.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Throwing inside the middleware: client sees a generic error, connection dies, no recovery path. Forgetting next() on success: connection hangs in the middleware queue forever.

task: WebAuthn registration ceremony: server-side challenge generation and verification of attestation.
reasoning: WebAuthn requires server to generate a random challenge, client signs with authenticator, server verifies. Library does heavy crypto; you wire up storage of credentials.
why_this_is_correct: Challenge stored server-side with short TTL — single-use, time-bounded. excludeCredentials prevents users registering the same authenticator twice. Public key + counter stored for later assertion. Rely on @simplewebauthn for crypto correctness.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting challenge from client: replay attacks. Long-lived challenges: replay window opens. Storing transports/counter wrong breaks subsequent authentication.

task: Server-render React 19 app with streaming using renderToPipeableStream to flush early shell while data loads.
reasoning: renderToPipeableStream streams HTML to client as it renders. Shell flushes first; Suspense boundaries show fallbacks until data resolves, then the resolved chunks stream in. Drops TTFB significantly.
why_this_is_correct: onShellReady fires when the synchronous shell is done — flush to user immediately for fast TTFB. Suspense boundaries below stream as they resolve. 10s abort cap protects against infinite renders. Client cleanup on close avoids dangling renders.
what_breaks_naive_approach: renderToString blocks until ALL data resolves — slow TTFB. No abort + slow data = held resources. Setting Content-Type after pipe = headers-already-sent crash.

task: Pattern match exhaustively over a discriminated union using a switch with assertNever default — and add a helper that returns the result.
reasoning: Different from arch-101: this one builds a `match` helper for fluent dispatch. Useful for value-returning consumers like rendering or formatting.
why_this_is_correct: CasesOf maps every kind to a handler with the narrowed variant — exhaustive by construction. Adding a new variant to Notification compiles only after adding the case to describe. assertNever fallback for the runtime edge of bad input.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain switch without exhaustive check: missing case silently returns undefined. if/else chain doesn't narrow well — TS sometimes loses the discriminator.

task: Broadcast to a room but skip sockets whose user has muted notifications.
reasoning: Default room broadcast hits all sockets. For filtered broadcasts, iterate fetchSockets() and emit per-socket conditionally. More CPU than blanket broadcast but precise.
why_this_is_correct: Parallel preference lookups; emit only to non-muted users. Returns counts for ops visibility. For very large rooms, batch preferences into a single bulk-lookup; this naive version is fine for small rooms.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sending blanket and filtering client-side: muted users still receive the network traffic and any UI badges before filtering. Querying prefs serially per socket: 100 sockets × 5ms lookup = 500ms total.

task: Verify a JWT issued by an external IdP (Auth0, Cognito) — check issuer, audience, and signature against JWKS.
reasoning: External IdPs publish public keys at a /.well-known/jwks.json endpoint. JWT 'kid' header tells you which key to use. Library jose handles the JWKS cache + verification.
why_this_is_correct: createRemoteJWKSet caches keys to avoid hitting IdP per request. Verifies issuer + audience to defeat token from another service or another tenant. Algorithm pinning blocks algorithm confusion. clockTolerance handles small clock drift.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Fetching JWKS on each request: per-request HTTP, rate-limited eventually. Not pinning issuer: another tenant's token accepted. Skipping audience: same IdP serves multiple services and tokens cross over.

task: asyncHandler one-liner that catches promise rejections and forwards to next.
reasoning: Express 5 catches async errors but the explicit wrapper is portable. Minimal, often the first thing teams add.
why_this_is_correct: Promise.resolve wraps non-promise returns. .catch(next) routes any rejection to Express error middleware. One line, no surprise behavior.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Unwrapped async handlers in Express 4: rejected promises become unhandled rejection warnings, eventually crash node. Manual try/catch in every route: boilerplate.

task: Fluent builder for HTTP client config with validation that prevents invalid combinations (no body on GET).
reasoning: Chainable methods set options; final build() validates. Type-state could enforce method-body constraint at compile time (covered in types-104); this uses runtime validation for simpler cases.
why_this_is_correct: Validations at build time catch invalid configs before sending. Fluent API reads naturally. Type-state version (types-104 pattern) would catch some of these at compile time.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain object literal: no validation, runtime errors deep in the request pipeline. Setting body on GET (some servers reject; others process it inconsistently) breaks unpredictably.

task: Redis pub/sub for cross-instance event broadcasting (server A publishes, all servers consume) with typed channels.
reasoning: Each instance subscribes to channels. Publish is fire-and-forget. Subscriber Redis connection is separate from command connection (subscriber-mode locks out other commands). Wrap with type-safe channel definitions.
why_this_is_correct: Separate publisher/subscriber clients required by Redis pub/sub semantics. Typed channel map gives autocomplete + payload typing. Unsubscribe when last handler leaves keeps Redis subscriptions minimal.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single Redis client trying to PUBLISH while in subscriber mode: error. Untyped messages: handlers receive 'unknown', easy to act on garbage. Forgetting unsubscribe leaks Redis subscriptions over time.

task: Build a typed Record-builder where keys come from a const tuple and values are explicit.
reasoning: Allows authoring config maps where missing keys are compile errors. Combined with `satisfies` (types-105) gives the best of literal narrowing + structural validation.
why_this_is_correct: K[number] is the literal union of tuple elements — must be exhaustive. Adding a role to ROLES forces rolePerms to add it. Runtime helper for the dynamic case; type-only helper for static literals.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Record<string, V>: any string key accepted, missing role silently undefined. Hand-typed `const rolePerms: { user, admin } = ...` drifts from the canonical roles list.

task: Admin endpoint that disconnects a specific user from all their Socket.IO sessions across the cluster.
reasoning: Use io.in(`user:${id}`).disconnectSockets() — adapter forwards to all instances. Pair with the per-user room (sock-112 pattern). Useful for security incidents.
why_this_is_correct: disconnectSockets(true) closes the underlying connection (preventing immediate reconnect with cached state). Redis/cluster adapter propagates to all instances so the user is disconnected from every pod. Pre-existing per-user room joins are required.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Iterating io.sockets.sockets and matching userId: only sees this server's sockets. Skipping the close flag may allow socket re-use depending on the adapter.

task: GraphQL endpoint has no depth/complexity limit — attacker sends deeply nested queries to DoS. Add limits.
reasoning: GraphQL queries can recurse fields exponentially (user → posts → user → posts...). Limit query depth + estimated complexity. Both at parse time before execution.
why_this_is_correct: Validation runs before execution — limit hits don't touch resolvers. Depth measured at the AST level so it accurately reflects what server will do. Combine with graphql-cost-analysis for field-weighted complexity.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No limit: nested query DoS, resolver overload, single request collapses worker. Counting depth at execution time wastes work before bailing.

task: Prioritize hydration of above-the-fold content over below-the-fold using React 19's prioritized hydration.
reasoning: React 19 hydrates Suspense boundaries in arrival order by default. To force order, structure your tree so above-fold content is outside Suspense (or in a Suspense that resolves immediately) and below-fold is in lazy Suspense.
why_this_is_correct: Article text is part of the shell — hydrates first, instantly interactive. Lazy boundaries hydrate as their chunks load. React 19 prioritizes hydration based on user interaction — clicking inside Comments while it's still hydrating preempts the order.
what_breaks_naive_approach: All components static-imported: monolithic bundle, single hydration pass blocks interaction. Wrapping the whole page in one Suspense boundary: nothing interactive until everything loads.

task: Process items concurrently with order preservation: results array matches input order even when fetches finish out-of-order.
reasoning: Promise.all on map(fn) preserves order automatically — that's its semantics. Often surprising to new devs who expect the order to depend on completion.
why_this_is_correct: Promise.all's contract: results[i] corresponds to the i-th input promise regardless of resolution order. Useful when you fan-out then need to recombine with positional context.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Pushing results as they resolve via Promise.race scrambles order. Manually tracking via a results[index] = await pattern works but is harder to read.

task: Detect when a client falls back from websocket to polling and warn them their connection is degraded.
reasoning: socket.conn.transport.name reveals current transport. Emit a 'connection:degraded' notice if polling is the only transport that worked.
why_this_is_correct: Initial check covers clients that never upgrade. upgrade event covers successful late upgrades. upgradeError covers attempted-but-failed upgrades (corporate proxy strip headers). Clients can show a 'degraded connection' badge.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Assuming all connections are websocket: polling clients still work but with higher latency; users blame the app. No upgrade visibility = silent degradation across the user base.

task: React component renders user-controlled style and on* attributes — strip them at the boundary.
reasoning: React itself blocks `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` event handlers, but rendering an arbitrary object as props is dangerous. Filter incoming attrs to an allowlist before spreading.
why_this_is_correct: Allowlist limits attrs to known-safe ones. Forbids `onClick` etc. (event handlers would be functions but defense-in-depth). Type check on values blocks objects that could carry attack payloads.
what_breaks_naive_approach: <div {...userAttrs}>: an attacker who controls a JSON config can set onClick={maliciousFn}. style attribute can carry url() expressions that exfiltrate via CSS.

task: Dynamic form with add/remove rows (e.g., line items in invoice) using useReducer.
reasoning: Array-of-rows state is awkward with multiple useState. Reducer with typed actions keeps the mutations predictable. Each row has stable id for React keys.
why_this_is_correct: Stable ids per row = React keys correct under add/remove. Reducer keeps mutations centralized. UPDATE with partial patch lets each field change independently.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Using array index as key: removing a row reuses state from the next row, inputs show wrong values. Multiple useState per row gets unwieldy.

task: Zod union errors are confusing. Build a helper that picks the most-relevant branch error to show users.
reasoning: Zod tries each union branch and accumulates errors. Default message lists all branches' errors. Better: pick the branch with the deepest match (most fields validated), show only its errors.
why_this_is_correct: Deepest path length = the branch the input most closely matched, so its errors are most actionable. Falls back to all issues if not a union error.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Showing all union errors confuses users with N variants' rules. Picking the first branch arbitrarily may show errors for a totally unrelated variant.

task: Lightweight plugin system: register hook functions for lifecycle events; plugins are loaded from a list.
reasoning: Plugin = exports a register function that subscribes to hooks. Core defines hook points. Plugins extend behavior without modifying core. Use TypedEmitter (types-202) under the hood for type safety.
why_this_is_correct: Plugins don't touch core code — they subscribe via the host. TypedEmitter prevents typo-name hooks. Duplicate name check prevents subtle double-registration bugs. log injected so plugins use the app's structured logger.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hardcoded calls to features (analytics, metrics, audit) inside core handlers = core grows monolithic. Untyped event bus invites typos.

task: Hidden sync I/O (fs.readFileSync, JSON.parseSync) blocks the event loop. Audit and replace with async equivalents.
reasoning: Sync I/O at startup is OK; sync I/O in request hot paths blocks all other requests. Audit by greppping for *Sync and `require`-time file reads. Cache results of expensive parses in module scope.
why_this_is_correct: Async readFile yields the event loop to other handlers during disk I/O. Caching avoids re-reading the same config on every request. Stream version for large files keeps memory bounded.
what_breaks_naive_approach: fs.readFileSync in a request handler: blocks event loop for tens of ms per call — degrades throughput linearly with traffic. Re-parsing JSON on every request: CPU overhead even when async.

task: Persist Mongo change-stream resume tokens to disk so a restart doesn't lose events.
reasoning: Resume tokens in memory die with the process. Persist to a small collection or file after each batch processed. On startup, read last token and resumeAfter.
why_this_is_correct: Resume token saved AFTER handler succeeds → at-least-once delivery (replay on restart, never skip). Per-cursor name allows multiple consumers (e.g., billing + analytics) with independent positions.
what_breaks_naive_approach: In-memory token: restart = miss all events during downtime. Saving token before handler: at-most-once (drop on crash mid-handler).

task: Detect when a socket's send buffer is growing and pause emits until drained.
reasoning: Use Socket.IO documented connection behavior rather than private write-buffer internals. Coalesce the latest telemetry frame per socket, use volatile emits for lossy high-frequency data, and resume when socket.conn emits drain.
why_this_is_correct: It bounds memory to one latest frame per socket, uses volatile emits for disposable telemetry, avoids compression on the hot path, and uses the documented drain event to resume writes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reading private connection buffer fields couples the app to internal Engine.IO state. Plain emit at 1kHz queues stale packets for slow clients until heap growth and GC pauses take down the process.

task: Differences between readonly array, readonly tuple, and ReadonlyArray<T> with practical examples.
reasoning: `readonly T[]` and `ReadonlyArray<T>` are identical for arrays. `readonly [...]` for tuples. const assertions widen `as const` give deeply readonly tuples. Choose based on intent.
why_this_is_correct: readonly tuple narrows to exact element types AND prevents mutation. EVENT_TYPES[number] gives the literal union. Mutable arrays are NOT assignable to readonly params, but readonly arrays ARE assignable to mutable params (variance) — defensive code copies before assigning.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain `number[]` parameter on a function called with readonly array: TS error, easy to over-mutate by accident. const-assertion missing on tuples widens to `string[]`, losing literal precision.

task: Hook that cancels in-flight fetch when component unmounts, using AbortController + ref.
reasoning: Common cleanup pattern. Without abort, the promise resolves on an unmounted component — setState warning + waste. AbortController in a ref persists across renders.
why_this_is_correct: Each call aborts the previous (search-box semantics). Unmount aborts in-flight. AbortError thrown on resolve so callers can ignore. Single controller per instance keeps lifecycle simple.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No abort: late response calls setState on unmounted component — React warning + wasted CPU. Single controller without per-call replacement only aborts the latest, leaving older ones hanging.

task: Add an FPS overlay (drei <Stats />) only in dev to monitor scene performance.
reasoning: drei Stats wraps stats.js. Mount in dev only to avoid shipping debug UI to production. Position via prop. Useful while tuning post-effects or geometry density.
why_this_is_correct: NODE_ENV check tree-shakes Stats from prod bundles. Single drop-in component shows FPS/MS/MB graphs. Pair with React DevTools Profiler for full picture.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Always-on Stats ships to users (debug UI, extra DOM, perf impact). Custom FPS counter via setInterval lies about actual render rate.

task: Load environment secrets at boot from a .env file (dev only) + verify required ones are set in prod.
reasoning: dotenv for local dev; in prod, use real secret manager. Fail fast if required env is missing in production. Never commit .env.
why_this_is_correct: dotenv only in dev (prod gets vars from secrets manager). Zod fails-fast at boot — pod doesn't start with bad config. Min length on JWT secret catches placeholder values. Optional sentry DSN doesn't kill boot if telemetry isn't configured.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reading process.env scattered in code: bad config crashes at first relevant request, not boot. Missing required env hidden until a code path needs it. Calling dotenv in prod hides 12-factor violations.

task: A new dev wraps every async route in try/catch. Show the asyncHandler abstraction that eliminates this.
reasoning: Repetitive try/catch obscures business logic. asyncHandler wraps once; rejected promises hit centralized error middleware.
why_this_is_correct: Handler body is the business logic, no try/catch. Throw a typed error; middleware translates to 4xx response. asyncHandler is one line — readable.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-route try/catch with res.status().json(): each route duplicates error shape, drifts. Forgetting one becomes an unhandled rejection in Express 4.

task: Map between persistence model (Mongo doc) and API DTO so API shape is decoupled from DB.
reasoning: Returning Mongo docs as API responses leaks DB schema (_id, __v, internal fields). Mapper functions translate Doc → DTO at the boundary. Reverse mapper for input (DTO → partial entity).
why_this_is_correct: Single place where DB shape meets API shape. passwordHash never reaches the DTO. createdAt as ISO string for JSON. Reverse mapper normalizes email and sets defaults. Refactoring the DB schema doesn't ripple to API consumers.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sending the Mongo doc directly: leaks passwordHash, __v, internal fields. Renaming a DB field forces an API change. No central mapper means every controller duplicates the same shaping logic.

task: Stream-parse a 5GB CSV upload without loading into memory. Pipeline: HTTP → CSV parser → batch insert.
reasoning: Streaming pipeline. Use csv-parse stream + a Writable that batches inserts. Backpressure handled by the pipeline. Errors propagated correctly.
why_this_is_correct: Pipeline propagates backpressure: parser pauses when writer is full. Batch insert reduces DB round trips. ordered:false continues past per-doc errors (e.g., duplicates) without breaking the batch. final flushes the remainder.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Buffering whole file: 5GB OOM. Insert-per-row: 5 million round trips, hours. No final hook: last partial batch is lost.

task: Broadcast a server-side event to every connected client at once.
reasoning: io.emit() reaches every connected socket across all namespaces' default namespace. For specific namespace: io.of('/foo').emit(). Use sparingly — broadcasts scale linearly.
why_this_is_correct: io.emit fans out to every connection. io.of('/path').emit scopes to a namespace. Pair with Redis adapter for multi-instance reach.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Iterating io.sockets.sockets and emitting per-socket = slower and not adapter-friendly. Forgetting namespace scoping leaks announcements across feature boundaries.

task: Envelope encryption with KMS: encrypt large data with a local key, encrypt the local key with KMS.
reasoning: KMS calls are slow and metered. Envelope encryption: generate a data key locally, KMS encrypts it. Store ciphertext + encrypted data key. Decrypt with one KMS call to retrieve data key, then bulk decrypt locally.
why_this_is_correct: AES-256-GCM for bulk encryption is fast (~GB/s). KMS only touches the small data key — fewer calls, less cost, lower latency. Zeroing data key after use reduces memory exposure window. Version field allows algorithm rotation.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Encrypting bulk data directly via KMS: each request is a KMS call + network roundtrip + size limit. Reusing data key across messages: same risks as IV reuse on a smaller scale. Never zeroing the data key keeps it in memory indefinitely.

task: Compound component pattern (Tabs.Root, Tabs.List, Tabs.Trigger, Tabs.Panel) with shared state via Context.
reasoning: Compound components let consumers compose UI flexibly while sharing parent-managed state. Parent owns active tab; children read via context. Headless UI / Radix use this pattern extensively.
why_this_is_correct: Context shares minimal state (activeTab). Consumers compose: <Tabs.Root>...<Tabs.Trigger id="a">A</Tabs.Trigger>... — flexible layout. ARIA attributes for accessibility. Validation throws if children are used outside Root.
what_breaks_naive_approach: <Tabs tabs={[...]} renderPanel={...} />: rigid API, can't reorder or interleave with arbitrary markup. Manual prop-passing for active state: every consumer wires the same plumbing.

task: Custom Sentry fingerprint so similar errors (different stack frames in user code, same root cause) group together.
reasoning: Sentry's default fingerprint is the top stack frame. Catches grouped differently for inline arrows or anonymous functions. Set a custom fingerprint based on error message + module path for better grouping.
why_this_is_correct: Normalizing message strips IDs/numbers so 'Order 12345 not found' and 'Order 67890 not found' group as one issue. Module + operation tags add operational context Sentry indexes for fast filtering. Custom fingerprint avoids the default 'one issue per stack hash' explosion.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default grouping creates one issue per unique stack frame combo: dashboards become noisy duplicates. Logging IDs into messages without normalization splits the same bug into thousands of issues.

task: Wire controllers via constructor injection in Express, separating route definition from handler classes.
reasoning: Class-based controllers with explicit deps make testing easy. Routes file just wires class instances to paths. No reaching into globals.
why_this_is_correct: Arrow class members preserve `this`. Constructor injection makes deps obvious and testable. Routes file is small and focused. Test by instantiating with a fake repo.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Static handlers reaching into global model imports: untestable without spinning up DB. Stuffing logic in routes file: no separation between transport and business.

task: Bulk audit-log writes don't need to confirm — use w:0 write concern to avoid waiting for acknowledgement.
reasoning: Default w:1 waits for primary to acknowledge. For low-importance high-volume writes (telemetry, audit logs that are duplicated elsewhere), w:0 fire-and-forget cuts latency in half. Trade-off: lost writes on failure.
why_this_is_correct: w:0 returns immediately after sending to server (no wait for ack). Save ~roundtrip latency. .catch handles transport errors. For critical audits, use w:'majority' instead — this is for lossy-acceptable high-volume cases like access logs.
what_breaks_naive_approach: w:'majority' on every audit log: latency adds up under load. Awaiting fire-and-forget defeats the purpose. Not catching the promise leaves unhandled rejection.

task: Pagination across a denormalized counter ('likes count') without skip(): use createdAt + _id cursor for stable ordering.
reasoning: Sort by likes desc + skip(N) is slow at high N. Cursor pagination = filter where (likes < lastSeen) OR (likes = lastSeen AND _id > lastId). Composite cursor preserves stable ordering when ties exist.
why_this_is_correct: Composite index on (likes, _id) supports both sort and cursor filter — covered query. $or handles ties (multiple posts with same likes count) deterministically using _id as secondary sort. Cursor is opaque to consumers (encode as base64 if exposing externally).
what_breaks_naive_approach: skip(1000): scans 1000 docs each page, gets slower as offset grows. Cursor on likes alone: ties cause skipped or duplicated rows across pages. No index = full collection scan.

task: Filter an array of mixed types and have TS narrow the result type via a user-defined type guard.
reasoning: `.filter` with a `value is T` predicate narrows the array's element type. Useful for stripping null/undefined or selecting one variant of a union.
why_this_is_correct: notNullish narrows away null/undefined. isOfKind narrows to the variant matching the discriminator. Chained filters compose without explicit `as` casts. Returned array typed precisely.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `.filter(x => x !== null)`: returns T | null array — null check inside .map needed even though we already filtered. Casting `as Notification[]` after filter masks logic bugs.

task: Versioned API endpoints (/v1, /v2) sharing authentication but with different response shapes.
reasoning: Express sub-routers per version. Shared middleware (auth, rate-limit) mounted before; per-version routes mounted after. Each version's controllers are independent — no shared mutation.
why_this_is_correct: Shared middleware applied to /api once. Versioned routers fully decoupled — v1 and v2 can evolve independently. 410 Gone on unmatched /api routes makes the version requirement explicit.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Branching shapes inside one handler with `if (req.path.startsWith('/v1'))`: spaghetti. Mounting auth inside each version: easy to forget. Removing v1 without 410 fallback leaves silent 404s confusing clients.

task: Rate-limit new Socket.IO connections per IP to defeat connection-flood attacks.
reasoning: Per-IP connection rate (e.g., 20 new connections/minute). Apply as io.use middleware that consults a Redis-backed bucket. Reject with descriptive error.
why_this_is_correct: Per-IP rolling counter. EXPIRE on first increment sets TTL. Rejection at handshake stops the connection before it consumes resources. Pair with sec-104 for IP allowlisting if NAT'd IPs are an issue.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No connection rate limit: attacker opens 10k connections in seconds, exhausts file descriptors. In-memory counter: doesn't span cluster.

task: Enable Trusted Types in browsers that support it to prevent DOM XSS from string-to-sink assignments.
reasoning: Trusted Types make innerHTML, eval, etc. require trusted objects instead of strings. Define a policy that sanitizes; assign policy-produced values to sinks. Browsers without support gracefully no-op.
why_this_is_correct: In TT-supporting browsers, only policy-vetted strings can hit innerHTML — catches typed-string mistakes at runtime. In other browsers, falls back to plain DOMPurify. Policy name registered in CSP header for enforcement.
what_breaks_naive_approach: node.innerHTML = userHtml without sanitization = XSS. Without TT, bugs that bypass DOMPurify aren't caught. Trusted Types in CSP without a policy breaks legitimate inline scripts/styles.

task: Native HTML5 drag-and-drop list reordering with React state.
reasoning: HTML5 DnD has odd API. Track dragged item id; on dragOver compute drop target; on drop reorder. Often easier via react-dnd, but native is dep-free.
why_this_is_correct: preventDefault on dragOver is required to enable drop. Reorder on dragOver gives live visual feedback. opacity-50 for the dragged item hints at action. Stable id-based key prevents React reordering bugs.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Skipping preventDefault: drop target never accepts drops. Reordering on drop only: no preview, jarring jump. Index-based keys: positions confuse React when items move.

task: Handlers sometimes call res.json() twice (e.g., in catch + finally). Detect and guard against 'cannot set headers after sent'.
reasoning: res.headersSent flag tells you if a response has started. Use it as a guard in catch blocks. Centralize via a safe response helper that no-ops on already-sent.
why_this_is_correct: headersSent guard prevents the crash. Logs the attempt so the underlying bug is visible. Wrapper class can be threaded through handlers if you want enforcement.
what_breaks_naive_approach: res.json() in try then res.status(500).json() in catch when try already responded but threw afterward: crash with 'cannot set headers'. The crash itself is a 500 to other in-flight responses if it tears down the worker.

task: Implement a Money value object with currency-aware arithmetic and structural equality.
reasoning: Primitive obsession bug: representing money as number loses currency context, allows USD + EUR addition. Wrap as VO; throw on mismatched currency; expose immutable operations.
why_this_is_correct: Integer minor units (cents) avoid float precision bugs. Currency check at construction. Operations return new instances (immutability). equals for structural comparison since JS === checks identity.
what_breaks_naive_approach: `number` for money: 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004. Adding USD + EUR silently produces a meaningless number. Mutable Money allows shared references to drift.

task: Long-running Node process has growing RSS even after explicit cleanup. Investigate native allocator fragmentation.
reasoning: glibc malloc fragments badly with mixed allocation sizes. Switching to jemalloc reduces RSS. Set via LD_PRELOAD or distro package. Not a Node-level fix but knowing about it saves days.
why_this_is_correct: Tracking RSS vs heapUsed reveals the fragmentation gap. heapUsed flat while RSS climbs = native allocator fragmentation. jemalloc preload typically halves RSS for mixed workloads. The TS code provides the observability that justifies the operational fix.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Looking only at V8 heap: glibc allocator overhead invisible. Adding more memory limit: postpones OOM but doesn't fix fragmentation. Restarting pods regularly is a workaround, not a fix.

task: Wrap optimistic-concurrency updates with automatic retry on version conflict, with bounded attempts and fresh read each retry.
reasoning: Conflict often indicates concurrent edits that don't actually conflict semantically. Retry: re-read, re-apply patch as a function, save again. Bounded attempts prevent infinite loop on permanent conflict.
why_this_is_correct: Patcher receives the latest state each attempt, so applied changes reflect current data. Exponential backoff between attempts reduces contention. Bounded attempts surface true conflicts as an error rather than silently spinning.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single-attempt OCC fails loudly on benign races (two unrelated field updates). Retrying without re-reading patches stale state. Unbounded retry can spin on a permanent conflict.

task: Replace `any` with `unknown` and show the narrowing patterns that make it ergonomic.
reasoning: unknown is `any`'s type-safe sibling: accepts anything but lets you do nothing without narrowing. Forces explicit checks. Combined with type guards (types-107) it's the right default for boundary code.
why_this_is_correct: unknown forces narrowing before use — TS catches missed checks at compile. Type guards encapsulate the narrowing. safeReadName never throws on bad input.
what_breaks_naive_approach: any propagates: `input.name.toLowerCase()` compiles and crashes when name isn't a string. JSON.parse() typed as any without narrowing infects downstream code.

task: Use socket.data with the SocketData generic so socket.data.userId is typed.
reasoning: socket.data is by default `any`. Server<…, …, …, SocketData> generic types it. Set in middleware, read in handlers with full type safety.
why_this_is_correct: SocketData generic gives socket.data full typing — no `(socket.data as any).userId`. Middleware sets fields with type checking. Handler access is typed throughout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Untyped Server generic: socket.data is any, typos compile, fields can be set inconsistently across middleware. Casts everywhere defeat the safety.

task: Webhook signature versioning: support both v1 (HMAC-SHA1) and v2 (HMAC-SHA256) during rollover.
reasoning: Old subscribers on v1, new on v2. Sign with both versions during transition. Verifier picks based on header. Eventually deprecate v1.
why_this_is_correct: Outbound emits both signatures so v1 and v2 subscribers both validate. requireV2 flag flips when rollout complete. Timing-safe compare prevents byte-leak. Timestamp check guards replay.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single-version cutover breaks every subscriber until they update. SHA1 alone is fine for backward compat but deprecated for new — supporting both via versioning is the migration path.

task: Virtualized list with sticky group headers (e.g., grouped by date).
reasoning: react-window doesn't natively support sticky headers. Track current group based on scroll position; render the sticky header outside the virtualized list with the current group label.
why_this_is_correct: Sticky label tracks the topmost visible group. Headers flatten into the row stream — same item size. Visible-index scan updates sticky label cheaply. Pure CSS sticky positions the header above the list.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Naive sticky CSS on group headers within virtualized list: react-window's positioning breaks sticky. Computing sticky based on full list lookups is O(n) per scroll tick — janks.

task: Fan out N async calls, each with its own timeout — collect all results (with timeouts as failures).
reasoning: Combine Promise.allSettled with per-call timeout wrapper. Each call's failure (including timeout) doesn't fail the batch. Returns mixed success/failure shape.
why_this_is_correct: Each call gets its own timeout — slow ones don't block others. Try/catch converts errors (including TimeoutError) into result shape. Promise.all is safe here because each inner promise resolves (never rejects). Order matches input.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Awaiting calls sequentially: total latency = sum of all. Promise.all without inner try/catch: first rejection cancels all results.

task: Provide the shared socket via React Context so deeply nested components can subscribe without importing the singleton.
reasoning: Combines sock-106 (singleton) with React Context (state-006). Provider wraps the app and exposes the socket; hooks read it. Easier mocking in tests.
why_this_is_correct: Production: defaults to getSocket() (real singleton). Tests: pass a mock Socket via the prop. Hook throws if used outside — clear failure mode.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Importing the singleton everywhere: tests must monkey-patch the module. Direct import couples components to the implementation.

task: Short-lived access token (5min) + long-lived refresh token (30d) flow with rotation on refresh.
reasoning: Short access tokens limit blast radius of leak. Refresh tokens stored httpOnly cookies; rotated on each use. Combine with sec-001 race fix.
why_this_is_correct: Access token short = limited damage if leaked. Refresh rotation makes a stolen old refresh useless. type claim prevents using access tokens as refresh (or vice versa). Path scoping on refresh cookie sends it only to /auth.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Long-lived access tokens: leak = days of access. No type claim: access token replayed as refresh accepted. Refresh not rotated: leak = unlimited access until expiry.

task: Hook that resolves an async value tied to a key (e.g., user details by id). When key changes, discard pending old result.
reasoning: Common bug: switch userId rapidly; old fetch resolves and overwrites new data. Same pattern as state-004 search — generation counter + abort.
why_this_is_correct: Generation counter ensures only the most recent fetch can write to state. Abort signal cancels in-flight network. Key in state matches the fetched data — consumers know the data is current.
what_breaks_naive_approach: No generation check: rapid key changes leave old data overwriting new. No abort: wastes bandwidth on cancelled requests. Storing only data without key: consumers can't tell if data matches the current request.

task: Last-resort handler that returns valid JSON 500 even when the rest of the error pipeline has failed.
reasoning: If your error middleware itself throws, Express falls back to default HTML 500. Wrap the error middleware in try/catch so the user never sees raw HTML.
why_this_is_correct: Catches errors in the error middleware itself (rare but real, e.g., serializing non-serializable object). headersSent check avoids double-send crash. Plain res.end() if even JSON fails.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Trusting the error middleware never throws: serializing a circular ref crashes it, Express returns default HTML 500, JSON-only clients break.
